coredata.dc:title:
Fabrication of N-GQDs and AgBiS2 dual-sensitized ZIFs-derived hollow ZnxCo3
-
xO4 dodecahedron for sensitive photoelectrochemical aptasensing of ampicillin
coredata.dc:creator: Yan, Tao
Zhang, Xue
Ren, Xiang
Lu, Yizhong
Li, Jinkai
Sun, Meng
Yan, Liangguo
Wei, Qin
Ju, Huangxian
coredata.dc:description: A sensitive photoelectrochemical (PEC) aptasensing platform based on Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and AgBiS2 dual-sensitized Zn/Co bimetallic oxide (ZnxCo3-xO4) dodecahedron as a matrix was constructed for the ultrasensitive detection of ampicillin (AMP). Concretely, the ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron derived from ZnCo-ZIFs retained the unique hollow porous structure, which provided large specific surface and efficacious active sites, and thereby significantly enhancing the light utilization and shortening the photoelectrons transmission path. Both N-GQDs with the up-conversion effect and eco-friendly AgBiS2 with optimal band gap for solar absorption were utilized to dual-sensitize ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron to further strengthen the PEC performance. Subsequently, the aptamer recognition technology was integrated with the modified photoelectrode, adopting AMP-aptamer to catch the target AMP, accompanied the hole oxidation of bind AMP causing the remarkable increase of photocurrent signal, the AMP could be specific and sensitive detect. Under optimized conditions, the photocurrent of developed aptasensor increased linearly displayed large liner response of 0.5 pmol/L – 10 nmol/L with a detection limit of 0.25 pmol/L (S/N = 3). Besides, the novel PEC AMP-sensoring exhibited excellent reproducibility, perfect stability and selectivity, which could open a potential avenue for antibiotic residues detection in environmental media.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bimetal organic framework
ZnxCo3-xO4/N-GQDs/AgBiS2
PEC aptasensor
Ampicillin

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref vitae

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of ZnCo-ZIFs and ZnCo-ZIFs derived ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron
Application of the PEC aptasensor in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The synthesis of ZCNGAB composite (A); the fabrication process of a PEC aptasensor for detection of AMP (B).

SEM images of ZnCo-ZIFs (A); ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron (inset: TEM image of ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron) (B); ZCNGAB (D); TEM and HRTEM images of ZCNGAB (C), (E), (F); XRD spectrum of (a) ZnCo-ZIFs, (b) ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron, (c) ZCNG, (d) ZCNGAB and (e) N-GQDs (G); UV–vis absorption spectrum of (a) N-GQDs, (b) ZCAB, (c) ZCNGAB (H); XPS patterns of ZCNGAB (I).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum (black line) and PL spectrum (red line, excitation at 330 nm) of a N-GQDs solution (insets: optical photographs of a N-GQDs solution under (a) normal light (b) UV lamp 365 nm); (B) Up-conversion PL spectra of a N-GQDs; (C) UV–vis DRS diffuse-reflectance spectra ZnxCo3-xO4 nanoparticles (a), ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron (b), ZCNG (c), ZCNGAB (d); (D) calculated band gap energies ZnxCo3-xO4 nanoparticles (a), ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron (b); (E) Mott-Schottky curves of samples ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron; (F) PL spectra of ZnxCo3-xO4 nanoparticles (a), ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron (b), ZCNG (c), ZCNGAB (d) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Schematic illustration of the photoelectrochemical aptasensor.

(A) Nyquist plots of electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of the modified ITO electrode and (B) Corresponding photocurrent of the modified ITO electrode: (a) ITO, (b) ZnxCo3-xO4/ITO, (c) ZCNG/ITO, (d) ZCNGAB/ITO, (e) Aptamer/ZCNGAB/ITO, (f) MCH/aptamer/ZCNGAB/ITO and (g) AMP/MCH/aptamer/ZCNGAB/ITO.

(A) Photocurrent intensity of modified electrode in the presence of different AMP concentrations (0.5 pmol/L to 10 nmol/L); (B) Logarithmic calibration curve determination of various concentrations of AMP; (C) Selectivity of the PEC aptasensor to AMP, (a) AMP, (b) AMP + tetracycline, (c) AMP + tobramycin, (d) AMP + amoxicillin, (e) tetracycline, (f) tobramycin, (g) amoxicillin; (D) The stability of the sensor under several switch lights cycles for 200 s.

head.ce:title:
Fabrication of N-GQDs and AgBiS2 dual-sensitized ZIFs-derived hollow ZnxCo3
-
xO4 dodecahedron for sensitive photoelectrochemical aptasensing of ampicillin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Interfacial Reaction & Sensing Analysis in Universities of Shandong, School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Department of Chemistry, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: d State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science, Department of Chemistry, Nanjing University, Nanjing 210023, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A sensitive photoelectrochemical (PEC) aptasensing platform based on Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) and AgBiS2 dual-sensitized Zn/Co bimetallic oxide (ZnxCo3-xO4) dodecahedron as a matrix was constructed for the ultrasensitive detection of ampicillin (AMP). Concretely, the ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron derived from ZnCo-ZIFs retained the unique hollow porous structure, which provided large specific surface and efficacious active sites, and thereby significantly enhancing the light utilization and shortening the photoelectrons transmission path. Both N-GQDs with the up-conversion effect and eco-friendly AgBiS2 with optimal band gap for solar absorption were utilized to dual-sensitize ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron to further strengthen the PEC performance. Subsequently, the aptamer recognition technology was integrated with the modified photoelectrode, adopting AMP-aptamer to catch the target AMP, accompanied the hole oxidation of bind AMP causing the remarkable increase of photocurrent signal, the AMP could be specific and sensitive detect. Under optimized conditions, the photocurrent of developed aptasensor increased linearly displayed large liner response of 0.5 pmol/L – 10 nmol/L with a detection limit of 0.25 pmol/L (S/N = 3). Besides, the novel PEC AMP-sensoring exhibited excellent reproducibility, perfect stability and selectivity, which could open a potential avenue for antibiotic residues detection in environmental media.

ce:section.ce:para: Ampicillin (AMP), a β-lactam antibiotic, has been widely used in various urinary systems and respiratory diseases caused by sensitive bacteria [1]. Meanwhile, ampicillin has also been commonly used in agriculture, resulting in residues in the environment [2]. Most seriously, due to the abuse of AMP, its long-term residual in the environment [3], thereby it caused many side effects on the human body, including allergic reactions and seizures [4]. Currently, various analytical techniques are available for AMP detection, including high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5], fluorescence [6], electrochemical [7] and chemiluminescence [8], et al. Although these methods are widely used to detect AMP, there are also some limitations, such as expensive equipment, complex operation, and specialized knowledge. Hence, it is an urgent need to develop a simple, inexpensive and rapid technology for AMP detecting.

Photoelectrochemical (PEC) aptasensor is a new type of analytical detection tool that combines the photoelectrochemical device and aptamer-specific recognition mode [9]. The determination of the analyte depends on the photoelectric conversion characteristics of the electrode material to a large extent, so the well-designed photoactive electrode with high photosensitivity and stability is its core component [10].

As an important subfamily of metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIFs) has been widely investigated due to its excellent performance [11–14]. In recent years, introducing different metal clusters into the ZIFs framework has become a strategy for the synthesis of bimetallic ZIFs [15–17]. Bimetallic ZIFs have two metal centers that enhance the stability of the skeletal structure, and the desired properties can be adjusted through the proportion of metal [18]. Recently, versatile catalyst with more active sites and high surface area that derived from ZIFs retains original structure and uniform elemental distribution, which has endowed widely applications in catalysis [19], sensors [20], supercapacitor [21], and so on. ZnCo-ZIFs derived ZnxCo3-xO4 as one of the outstanding has been initially explored in photocatalysis. Nowadays, Li’s group reported that ZnCo-ZIFs was treated by oxidation, sulfurization and phosphorylation, and then Pt was doped to prepare a heterojunction photocatalyst for hydrogen production [22]. Afterwards, Zhang’s group prepared CdS nanoparticles decorated ZnCo-ZIFs derived hollow ZnxCo3-xO4 for effective photocatalytic water evolution [23]. Besides, Zhao’ group discussed the hollow porous ZnxCo3-xO4 nanocrystalline derived from ZnCo-ZIFs for the photodegradation of gaseous toluene [24]. From the above, multiple studies have demonstrated that the ZnCo-ZIFs derived hollow porous ZnxCo3-xO4 has excellent photoactivity, implicating its proposed use as photoactive material for PEC sensing platform construction.

AgBiS2, is a non-toxic, earth-abundant, and environmental-friendly ternary chalcogenides with the band gap is 1.2 eV [25], which is similar to the optimal band gap for solar absorption (1.39 eV). Due to its high thermal stability, excellent optical and electrical properties, AgBiS2 has been commonly used as thermoelectric and optoelectronic materials [26]. As is well-known, the CdS, CdTe, In2S3 etc. acted as eminent sensitizers have been widely adopted in PEC device, however, the inherent toxicity and expensive cost are fatal flaw for promising PEC applications. Inspiringly, it is significative that AgBiS2 is adopted to exploit the potential as photo-sensitizer for PEC sensing matrix. Meanwhile, Nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) is an excellent electron transfer carrier [27] with the up-conversion effect. In particular, the introduction of nitrogen provides more active sites, leading to its more efficient and practical application for PEC sensing [28].

Based on the above studies, a novel ZIFs-derived ZnxCo3-xO4/N-GQDs/AgBiS2 dodecahedron was successfully fabricated for the first time with superior PEC properties: (i) the hollow nanostructure can facilitate light harvesting due to multiple refractions of light within the inner [24]; (ii) the porous thin shell formed after the annealing provided a shorter transport path for electronics, thereby promoting carrier transfer and separation [29]; (iii) the high carrier transport mobility and up-conversion effect of N-GQDs along with the prominent visible light absorption of AgBiS2 were utilized to sensitize ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron, greatly improving the PEC performance. Following, a label-free PEC aptasensor was developed for AMP detection using ZnxCo3-xO4/N-GQDs/AgBiS2 as photoactive matrix. The specific AMP-blinding aptamer was employed to catch AMP through the affinity reaction of AMP molecular side chain with AMP aptamer secondary structure [30]. The concentration of AMP was determined by observing a rising photocurrent signal, which caused through the oxidation of captured AMP by photo-generated holes. The well-designed aptasensor showed satisfactory sensitivity, selectivity and stability, which will provide inspiration for its candidate method for environmental residual pollutants detection.
AMP aptamer with the following sequences: 5′-NH2-(CH2)6- TTT TGC GGG CGG TTG TAT AGC GG -3′ was obtained from Sangon Biotech Co., Ltd.
Other materials, reagents and apparatus are shown in the supporting materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of ZnCo-ZIFs and ZnCo-ZIFs derived ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron
ce:section.ce:para: The ZnCo-ZIFs and ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron were prepared as described previously [22]. Briefly, 297 mg Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, 291 mg Co(NO3)2·6H2O and 30 mL methanol were mixed under continuous stirring. 328 mg 2-methylimidazole was added into 10 mL methanol. The two solutions were then mixed and subsequently maintained at room temperature for 24 h. The product was separated by centrifugation, washed with methanol three times and dried at 60 ℃ for 12 h. Then ZnCo-ZIFs was transferred into a muffle oven, and calcinated at 300 ℃ for 3 h. The obtained ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron was cooled down naturally and ground to powder for further use.
As a comparison, traditional ZnxCo3-xO4 nanoparticles were synthesized according to reference with some modification [31]. The details were exhibited in the Supporting information.
The N-GQDs were synthesized according to previous report [28]. The details were exhibited in the Supporting information.

Before constructing the ZCNGAB electrodes, indium-tin-oxide (ITO) substrates (1.8 × 0.5 cm2) were ultrasonically treated by acetone, ethanol, ultrapure water. The fabrication process of ZCNGAB electrodes was shown in Scheme 1 A. Firstly, 10 μL ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron solution was introduced onto ITO electrode until dry out. Then, the ZnxCo3-xO4/ITO was immersed in 2% (V/V) APTES methanol solution for a night to assemble the -NH2 group, the obtained APTES-ZnxCo3-xO4/ITO was then immersed in a solution of the N-GQDs with EDC/NHS, followed maintain for 2 h. The deposit of AgBiS2 onto the surface of ZnxCo3-xO4/N-GQDs/ITO (ZCNG/ITO) was decorated through a SILAR method. The ZCNG/ITO electrode was dipped in 0.1 mol/L AgNO3 ethanol solution for 30 s, then immersed in 0.1 mol/L Bi(NO3)3 ethanol solution (volume 1:1) for 30 s, the electrode was rinsed with ethanol for each step. Lastly, the obtained electrode was immersed in 0.1 mol/L Na2S methanol/water solution (volume 1:1) for 60 s and washed with methanol. Cycle this process multiple times and calcined at 150 °C for 1 h.

In brief, 5 μL of thioglycolic acid (TGA) (0.1 mol/L) was introduced onto the ZCNGAB/ITO electrode for 0.5 h and then washed with buffer (PBS). Subsequently, 5 μL of EDC/NHS solution was dropped on the TGA-ZCNGAB/ITO electrode for 0.5 h to activate −COOH of TGA. After washed thoroughly, 5 μL aptamer (dispersed in 0.01 mol/L PBS) was dropped onto the electrode and incubated for 2 h, then cleaned up with buffer to remove physically bound aptamers. Whereafter, non-specific sites were blocked by dropping 5 μL of MCH for 0.5 h. Finally, 5 μL AMP solutions was dispersed on modified electrode for another 1 h, the finished electrode rinsed slightly with buffer. Thus, the PEC aptasensor for specific detection of AMP has been successfully constructed (Scheme 1B).

The PEC experiments were operated at an electrochemical workstation (CHI 760E Chenhua Instrument Company, Shanghai, China). The photocurrent was measured in PBS (pH = 7.4) with a three-electrode system, the xenon lamp (300 W, λ > 420 nm) was as an excitation source and the applied potential was 0.5 V (vs. SCE). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis was performed in a 2.5 mmol/L [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− (0.1 mol/L KCl) solution. The frequency range was 0.1–105 Hz with amplitude of 5 mV.

The SEM and TEM images were used to demonstrate the e morphology of the synthesized materials. As shown in Fig. 1 A, ZnCo-ZIFs was a regular polyhedron having a diameter of about 300 nm and its surface was relatively smooth. After annealing, the ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron retained the shape and size of ZnCo-ZIFs, but the surface became rough (Fig. 1B). It's worth noting that some pores appeared on the surface of the hollow structural shell. Furthermore, Fig. S1 revealed the N-GQDs with size of about 5 nm and the lattice spacing of 0.23 nm. As observed from Fig. 1C and D, it can be seen that the polyhedron structure of ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron became indistinct after modified by N-GQDs and AgBiS2, and its surface was covered with nanoparticles less than 20 nm in diameter. The HRTEM images of the ZCNGAB showed that lattice spacing distances of 0.21 nm and 0.28 nm correspond to the (110) planes of the N-GQDs and the (200) palnes of the AgBiS2 (Fig. 1E, F), respectively, indicating the successful synthesis of ZCNGAB. The tight contact between each component could facilitate the transfer of photo-generated electrons. Besides, the elemental mapping images (Fig. S2) of ZCNGAB showed that C, O, N, Co, Zn, Ag, Bi, and S elements clearly distinguished.

The XRD patterns of the synthesized materials were shown in Fig. 1G, the pattern of ZnCo-ZIFs coincided with previous report (curve a) [32]. For the XRD pattern of the sample after calcination, characteristic peaks of ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron appeared in 31.2°, 36.8°, 44.7°, 59.2°, 65.1°(curve b), which could be assigned to the (200), (311), (400), (511) and (440) diffraction planes (JCPDS No.23–1390), and accompanied by the disappearance of peaks of ZnCo-ZIFs. Besides, the weak peaks at 34.4°, 47.5°, and 62.8° were attributed to the ZnO phase, demonstrating that ZnCo-ZIFs has been completely translated to metal oxide by calcination [24]. After introduced N-GQDs, there were no distinct characteristic peaks due to the low dosage of N-GQDs. As for ZCNGAB, three pronounced diffraction peaks at 27.5°, 31.7° and 45.7°, which can be indexed to the (111), (200), and (220) crystal planes of AgBiS2 phase (JCPDS No. 21–1178), respectively, indicating the ZCNGAB composites were successfully synthesized. As were presented in Fig. 1H, N-GQDs showed characteristic absorption peaks at about 235 nm and 330 nm, these peaks were also found in ZCNGAB, indicating the successful preparation of composite materials. For comparison, the SEM and XRD of ZnxCo3-xO4 nanoparticles was also conducted (Fig.S7).

XPS was used as a technique to analyze the surface chemical state of prepared sample. The full XPS spectrum of ZCNGAB in Fig. 1I presented the elements of Zn, Co, O, N, C, Ag, Bi and S appeared. The other analyses were described in the Supporting information (Fig.S3).

Fig. 2 A demonstrated the UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-GQDs, the absorption band at 235 nm was due to π-π* transitions, and the absorption band at 330 nm corresponded to n-π* transitions [33]. Besides, when irradiated with 365 nm UV light, blue light was observed in the N-GQDs solution, which can be put down to photoluminescence characteristics of N-GQDs. It followed that N-GQDs have a certain visible light absorption capacity. When excited at 330 nm, this solution exhibited a stronger maximum emission at 445 nm. As observed in Fig. 2B, N-GQDs excited by long-wavelength (680−840 nm) light emit visible light around 440−470 nm, which was attributed to the up-conversion luminescence effect of N-GQDs.

From Fig. 2C, the UV–vis diffuse-reflectance spectra of ZnxCo3-xO4 nanoparticle, ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron, ZCNG, and ZCNGAB were exhibited. The absorption edge of ZnxCo3-xO4 nanoparticle and ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron were estimated at 461 nm and 480 nm, respectively. The enhanced light absorption of ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron due to the hollow porous structure exposes more active sites, while multiple refractions of light within the ZIFs derived structure increase light utilization. The ZCNG showed red-shift and enhanced light absorption, emphasizing the pivotal role of N-GQDs as photosensitizers. After the deposition of AgBiS2, the obtained composites further enhanced the visible light absorption capacity. For comparison, as shown in Fig. 2D, the band-gap energy of ZnxCo3-xO4 nanoparticle and ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron were measured as 2.69 eV and 2.58 eV by the linear region of the extrapolation graph, indicating more efficient utilization of visible light.

Mott-Schottky (M–S) plot was used to characterize the flat band potential (E fb) and conduction band potential (E CB) of the as-prepared material. The M–S plot in Fig. 2E determined ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron was n-type semiconductor [34], the E fb was obtained by the intercept on the X axis of the tangent to the M–S plot. Therefore, the E fb of ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron was about −0.54 V vs. SCE (−0.3 V vs. NHE). According to the literature, the E fb of n-type semiconductors is more negative than E CB by about 0.1−0.2 V [35]. Hence, it can be calculated that EvB and E CB of ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron were about −0.4 V and 2.18 V (vs. NHE) respectively, which was similar to literature reports [32,36]. In addition, the E VB and E CB of N-GQDs and AgBiS2 could be obtained from literatures [26,37].

Photoluminescence (PL) spectra was adopted to illustrate the law of charge transfer and migration efficiency. The low photoluminescence intensity represented the low composite efficiency of electrons and holes [38]. When excited at 270 nm, a highest emission peak at 395 nm was detected (Fig. 2F), corresponding to ZnxCo3-xO4, but the intensity of ZnxCo3-xO4 nanoparticles was much stronger than ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron, demonstrating that the annealed dodecahedral porous thin shells provides large specific surface and efficacious active sites, and thereby shortens the electron transport path. The emission peak of ZCNG was significantly weakened due to the prominent electron conduction function of N-GQDs. Besides, the emission peak of ZCNGAB nearly disappeared, which could be attributed to the synergistic effect of N-GQDs and AgBiS2 further improving the photoelectron transfer, which was crucial for the PEC sensing.

Fig. 3 schematically illustrated the photo-generated electrons and holes transfer mechanism of PEC aptasensor. While exposed under visible light, the photogenerated electrons were excited and generated the electron-hole pairs. Due to the CB of AgBiS2 was more positive than N-GQDs, the photogenerated electrons could be easily injected into AgBiS2. Soon after, the photogenerated electrons were injected into ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron because of the most negative CB energy level. Meanwhile, the holes remained in ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron would transfer to the VB of AgBiS2 and N-GQDs, and then transmitted to the solid-liquid interface between the electrode and the electrolyte. The E VB of AgBiS2 and N-GQDs were +0.58 V and +0.76 V (vs. NHE), respectively, which could not oxidize OH− (OH−/·OH=+1.89 V vs. NHE) and H2O (H2O/·OH = +2.72 V vs. NHE) into ·OH [39].Consequently, the photogenerated holes remained in VB can directly oxidized AMP molecules captured by aptamer, inhibited recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes, resulting an increased photocurrent [4].

EIS testing can be used as a means of explaining the electrode construction process, the semicircle diameter of the EIS Nyquist diagram reflects the electrode transfer resistance (R et) value [40]. As demonstrated in Fig. 4 A, bare ITO showed the smallest semicircle (curve a, R et = 48.39). When ZnxCo3-xO4 dropped on ITO the R et increased significantly (curve b, R et = 71.74), demonstrated that ZnxCo3-xO4 has been tightly integrated with ITO. After decoration of N-GQDs and AgBiS2, the diameter of the semicircle increased to 109.7 Ω and 117.8 Ω, respectively (curve c and d), these results proved that the successful modification of N-GQDs and AgBiS2. Subsequently, after incubating the aptamer, the value of R et was further increased (curve e, R et = 141), which was attributed the phosphate backbone with negatively charged aptamer molecules, repelled with [Fe(CN)6]3−/4−probe [41], meanwhile, it demonstrated that aptamer were fixated by amidation reaction [28]. Furthermore, with the modification of the MCH, the steric hindrance was more apparent due to its low conductivity, and the value of R et increased (curve f, R et = 177.2). Lastly, the value of R et increased significantly due to the complex, which formed between the aptamer and the AMP, further prevented the [Fe(CN)6]3−/4− probe from reaching the electrode for electron transfer (curve g, R et = 297.4) [42]. The values of R s, Z ω, C dl and R et were shown in Table S1.

The time-based photocurrent response can further elucidate the successful construction of photoelectrode. As displayed in Fig. 4B, bare ITO exhibited minimal photocurrent, almost negligible (curve a). Slightly increased photocurrent responses (curve b) were obtained after introducing ZnxCo3-xO4 on ITO. After modifying N-GQDs on ZnxCo3-xO4/ITO, the intensity of the photocurrent continued to increased (curve c), demonstrated that the possessed good electrical conductivity and up-conversion effect. When AgBiS2 was deposited by the SILAR method, the photocurrent exhibited peak value (curve d). These results proved that the synergistic effect of the formed AgBiS2/ZnxCo3-xO4 heterojunction and N-GQDs as conductive frameworks, promoting the transfer and separate of charge carriers, and uttermost enhanced the photoelectric response of the PEC device. Subsequently, with the successive conjugation of aptamer and MCH, the photocurrent decreased (curve e and f), which was due to aptamer as protein molecules and blocking effect of MCH hindered the transfer of interface electrons. However, when the AMP were coated on the modified electrode, the aptamer was specifically bound to the AMP, at which photogenerated holes reacted with the AMP, thereby generating an elevated photocurrent (curve g). All the above results proved the successful construction of the aptasensor.

In addition, as shown in Fig S4, in the absence of aptamer, the photocurrent response changed inconspicuous, indicating the less amount of AMP molecules could be adsorbed on the surface of ZCNGAB/ITO, and more likely to fall off from the surface during the washing step. These results illustrate that the outstanding advantages of PEC aptasensor are the high affinity and specificity [43]. Definitely, the specific binding of aptamer can fasten more analyte for PEC sensing, and therefore realizing the high sensitivity and excellent selectivity of sensor.

In the preparation of the modified electrode, AgBiS2 was deposited by the SILAR process, so the number of SILAR cycles was a key factor affecting the intensity of photocurrent. As shown in Fig. S5A, when the SILAR cycles number was 3, the photocurrent reached maximum. Which was due to excessively loaded AgBiS2 blocking the active site, thereby inhibiting the utilization of solar light. When applied potential changed between 0.2 – 0.9 V, photocurrent reached peak at 0.5 V. Thus, 0.5 V (vs. SCE) was used in this work (Fig. S5B).

Fig. S5C exhibited the photocurrent intensity of the aptasensor changed with the pH of the buffer solution, reaching a maximum at pH = 7.4, because that the pH of 7.4 simulated the neutral environment of the physiological, which preserved the biological activity of the protein [44].

The effect of aptamer concentrations was examined, and showed in Fig. S5D, the photocurrent intensity decreased with the increase of aptamer concentration, when the aptamer concentration exceeded 1.5 μmol/L, the photocurrent nearly reached a steady. Therefore, 1.5 μmol/L was selected when constructing PEC aptasensor.

Under optimal conditions, the fabricated PEC aptasensor was applied to detect various concentrations of AMP for rigorous quantitative determination. Fig. 5 A displayed the concentrations of AMP were in the range of 0.5 pmol/L – 10 nmol/L, as the AMP concentration increased, the photocurrent increased with regularity. As illustrated in Fig. 5B, the linear relationship between the photocurrent and the logarithmic values of AMP concentration, the linear equation was I =2.1653 lgc + 10.15 (R 2 = 0.994), and the LOD was 0.25 pmol/L (S/N = 3). The proposed PEC aptasensor possessed broader liner response and lower detection limit among the reported methods for AMP determination (table S2).

Selectivity was a key investigation condition to verify the performance of PEC aptasensor. Three antibiotics including tetracycline, tobramycin and amoxicillin were selected as interference agents for interference experiments. As depicted in Fig. 5C, when 100-fold of interfering substances were added to the AMP solution, there were no significant change in the photocurrent. When the detection is merely interfering agent, MCH blocked non-specific binding on the electrode surface, it did not show significant PEC response, which suggested the high selectivity of the PEC aptasensor.

The stability was an important criterion for measuring PEC aptasensor performance. Fig. 5D exhibited the photocurrent of 20 times on/off irradiation cycles in 200 s, no significant fluctuations in the repeat photocurrent response were observed in the experiment, indicating that the PEC signal readout was stable.

Under optimal conditions, the photocurrent was tested to illuminate the reproducibility of five ZnxCo3-xO4/ITO electrodes and five modified electrodes with 0.1 nmol/L AMP. As displayed in Fig. S6A and B, the RSD value was 1.9 % and 2.2 %, respectively, indicating that the fabricated aptasensor has satisfactory reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the PEC aptasensor in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The practicability of the prepared aptasensor were detected by standard addition method, different AMP concentration (1, 5, 10 nmol/L) in tap water and lake water were as samples. The recovery range was 98.4%–109.8%, and the RSD was 1.7%–3.5% (Table S3), confirming the feasibility and practicability of the designed PEC aptasensor in real samples.

In brief, ZnxCo3-xO4/N-GQDs/AgBiS2 composite was successfully synthesized for the first time to construct PEC aptasensing platform for AMP detection. The unique hollow porous ZnxCo3-xO4 dodecahedron obtained by annealing process of ZnCo-ZIFs enhanced the light utilization, and provided a more efficient transmission path for photoelectrons. Following the integration of N-GQDs and AgBiS2 not only broaden the absorption of visible light, but also further accelerate the transfer of electrons. Benefiting from the cleverly adopted AMP aptasensing strategy, the bind AMP molecule was oxidized by the photogenerated hole, which significantly enhanced the photocurrent signal, and therefore the AMP could be sensitive detect. The constructed PEC aptasensor expressed low detection limit (0.25 pmol/L) and large linear range (0.5 pmol/L – 10 nmol/L) for AMP detection. The simple, stable, highly selective and sensitive detection platform demonstrated its broad application prospects in the field of environmental detection.

Tao Yan: Conceived and Designed the experiments; Xue Zhang and Xiang Ren: Conceptualization, Data curation and Writing - original draft; Yizhong Lu and Jinkai Li: Methodology and Writing - review & editing; Meng sun: Formal analysis and Methodology; Liangguo Yan: Investigation; Qin Wei and Huangxian Ju: Funding acquisition and Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Key Scientific Instrument and Equipment Development Project of China (No. 21627809), National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21505051, 21575050, 21607055, 21777056), Jinan Scientific Research Leader Workshop Project (2018GXRC024), the Youth Creative Talents Introduction and Cultivation Plan in Universities of Shandong Province. Ju thanks the Special Foundation for Taishan Scholar Professorship of Shandong Province (No. ts201712052) and UJN.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128387.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tao Yan received the pH.D. degree from Beijing Institute of Technology (BIT), Beijing, China. He is an associate professor of School of Resource and Environment at University of Jinan, Shandong, China. His research areas focus on the construction of photoelectric functional materials for biosensor, energy and environment applications.
Xue zhang studies in school of resources and environment, University of Jinan as postgraduate student.

Xiang Ren received his B.S. degrees in Chemistry of Materials/English from University of Jinan in 2012, M.S. degree in Chemical Engineering and Technology from University of Jinan in 2015, and pH.D. degree from University of Jinan/University of Electronic Science and Technology of China in 2019. His research areas focus on energy catalysis, nanomaterials controlled-synthesis, and electrochemical biosensors.

Yizhong Lu received the pH.D. from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2015. He then worked as a research fellow at Nanyang Technological University & Cambridge Center for Advanced Research in Energy Efficiency in Singapore. Currently, he is a full professor in the School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Shandong. His research areas focus on size-controlled synthesis, characterization, and size-dependent property study of metal nanocusters, nanobiosensing, and electrochemisty.
Jinkai Li received the pH.D. degree from Northeastern University, Shenyang, China. His research areas focus on the synthesis and application of rare earth luminescent materials
Meng Sun received the pH.D. degree from Fuzhou University, Fujian, China. His research areas focus on the design and application of new photocatalytic material in water pollution control.
Liangguo Yan received the pH.D. degree from Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, China. His research areas focus on the preparation of nanomaterials and sorption processes for heavy metals.

Qin Wei, a professor and DSc, has devoted herself to analytical teaching and scientific research. Her main research interests are the determination of protein and nucleic acid by photometry and the electrochemical immunosensor preparation. She has published over one hundred articles on analysis, immunosensor and applied successfully for many research projects, such as Biomaterials, Adv. Funct. Mater., Biosens. Bioelectron., Sens. Actuators B: Chem., Talanta.

Huangxian Ju received his BS, MS and pH.D. degrees from Nanjing University during 1982–1992. He was a postdoc in Montreal University (Canada) from 1996 to 1997 and a guest professor in three universities of Germany and Ireland in 1999−2000. He became an associate and full professor of Nanjing University in 1993 and 1999. He is currently the director of State Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Life Science. His research interests focus on analytical biochemistry, biosensing and molecular diagnosis. He has published 616 papers in different journals with SCI h-index of 83 (29,523 citations) and Google Scholar h-index of 91 with more than 29,000 citations.
